title: Design a URL Shortener
description: Design a URL shortening service like TinyURL. Given a long URL, generate a short unique URL. Given a short URL, redirect to the original long URL. Consider how you'd handle collisions and scale.
hints: Think about the core operations: encode (long to short) and decode (short to long). What data structure maps between them?
A hash map works. For generating short codes, consider base62 encoding of an auto-increment ID or a hash of the URL.
Use a counter-based ID converted to base62 for the short code. Store bidirectional mappings. For scale, think about database sharding and caching.
follow_ups: How would you handle high traffic?

title: Design an LRU Cache
description: Design and implement a Least Recently Used cache. It should support get(key) and put(key, value) operations in O(1) time. When the cache reaches capacity, evict the least recently used item.
hints: You need O(1) lookup AND O(1) ordering updates. Which two data structures together achieve this?
Hash map for O(1) lookup. Doubly linked list for O(1) move-to-front and remove-from-end.
Hash map stores key -> DLL node. On get: move node to front. On put: add to front, if over capacity remove from tail.
follow_ups: How would you make it thread-safe?
How would you distribute it across servers?

title: Design a Rate Limiter
description: Design a rate limiter that limits the number of requests a client can make within a given time window. For example, allow at most 100 requests per minute per user.

examples.input: limit = 100 requests per 60 seconds, user makes 101st request

hints: Think about how you track the number of requests in a sliding time window.
Token bucket or sliding window counter are common approaches. Token bucket: tokens refill at a fixed rate, each request costs one token.
Sliding window: store timestamps of recent requests. On new request, remove timestamps older than the window, check if count < limit.
follow_ups: Token bucket vs sliding window — trade-offs?
How would you make it distributed?
How do you handle burst traffic?
